The natural sciences of psychology, neuroscience, and ethology, the engineering sci-\nences of artiﬁcial intelligence, optimal control theory, and operations research, and the social sciences of economics and\nanthropology—all focus in part on intelligent decision makers. The perspectives of the various disciplines are different,\nbut they have common elements. One cross-disciplinary goal is to identify the common core—those aspects of the deci-\nsion maker that are common to all or many of disciplines. To the extent that such a common model of the decision maker\ncan be established, the exchange of ideas and results may be facilitated, progress may be faster, and the understanding\ngained may be more fundamental and longer lasting.\n\nThe quest for a common model of the decision maker is not new. One measure of its current vitality is the success of\ncross-disciplinary meetings such as RLDM and the Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), and\njournals such as Neural Computation, Biological Cybernetics, and Adaptive Behavior.
